section.title: The Servant of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: This chapter contains the first of Isaiah's Servant Songs, verses 1 to 9, one of the Book of Comfort's most important passages, introducing a gentle, Spirit-filled Servant who will bring justice to the nations without breaking a bruised reed. The chapter then turns to contrast this ideal Servant with Israel's own blindness and deafness, showing the gap between the calling and the nation's actual failure to keep it.
section.thesis: The LORD presents His chosen Servant, gentle enough not to break a bruised reed yet faithful enough to establish justice on the earth, as a covenant for the people and a light to the nations, and then exposes that Israel itself, called to this very role, has been blind and deaf, unable to fulfill the calling only the true Servant can complete.
section.frameNote: Six movements: the first Servant Song introducing the gentle, justice-bringing Servant; His mission as covenant and light to the nations; a new song of praise summoned from all the earth; the LORD roused to act like a warrior and a woman in labor; the searching question of who is blind but God's own servant; and the closing lament over a plundered, uncomprehending people.

verses.text: “Here is My {{ebed|Servant}}, whom I uphold, My Chosen One, in whom My soul delights. I will put My Spirit on Him, and He will bring {{mishpat|justice}} to the nations.
He will not cry out or raise His voice, nor make His voice heard in the streets.
A bruised reed He will not break and a smoldering wick He will not extinguish; He will faithfully bring forth justice.
He will not grow weak or discouraged before He has established justice on the earth. In His law the islands will put their hope.”

units.label: A covenant for the people, a light to the nations

verses.text: This is what God the LORD says, He who created the heavens and stretched them out, who spread out the earth and its offspring, who gives breath to the people on it and life to those who walk in it:
“I, the LORD, have called you for a righteous purpose, and I will take hold of your hand. I will keep you and appoint you to be a covenant for the people and a light to the nations,
to open the eyes of the blind, to bring prisoners out of the dungeon and those sitting in darkness out from the prison house.
I am the LORD; that is My name! I will not yield My glory to another or My praise to idols.
Behold, the former things have happened, and now I declare new things. Before they spring forth I proclaim them to you.”

units.label: Sing to the LORD a new song
units.note: praise from the ends of the earth

verses.text: Sing to the LORD a new song, His praise from the ends of the earth, you who go down to the sea, and all that is in it, you islands, and all who dwell in them.
Let the desert and its cities raise their voices; let the villages of Kedar cry aloud. Let the people of Sela sing for joy; let them cry out from the mountaintops.
Let them give glory to the LORD and declare His praise in the islands.
The LORD goes forth like a mighty one; He stirs up His zeal like a warrior. He shouts; yes, He roars in triumph over His enemies:

units.label: I will groan like a woman in labor

verses.text: “I have kept silent from ages past; I have remained quiet and restrained. But now I will groan like a woman in labor; I will at once gasp and pant.
I will lay waste the mountains and hills and dry up all their vegetation. I will turn the rivers into dry land and drain the marshes.

I will lead the blind by a way they did not know; I will guide them on unfamiliar paths. I will turn darkness into light before them and rough places into level ground. These things I will do for them, and I will not forsake them.
But those who trust in idols and say to molten images, ‘You are our gods!’ will be turned back in utter shame.

units.label: Who is blind but My servant?

verses.text: Listen, you deaf ones; look, you blind ones, that you may see!
Who is blind but My servant, or deaf like the messenger I am sending? Who is blind like My covenant partner, or blind like the servant of the LORD?
Though seeing many things, you do not keep watch. Though your ears are open, you do not hear.”
The LORD was pleased, for the sake of His righteousness, to magnify His law and make it glorious.
But this is a people plundered and looted, all trapped in caves or imprisoned in dungeons. They have become plunder with no one to rescue them, and loot with no one to say, “Send them back!”
Who among you will pay attention to this? Who will listen and obey hereafter?
Who gave Jacob up for spoil, and Israel to the plunderers? Was it not the LORD, against whom we have sinned? They were unwilling to walk in His ways, and they would not obey His law.
So He poured out on them His furious anger and the fierceness of battle. It enveloped them in flames, but they did not understand; it consumed them, but they did not take it to heart.

ebed.gloss: Servant, one who serves under another's authority, sometimes with great honor, as of Moses or David. Here it introduces the first of Isaiah's Servant Songs, a figure the LORD upholds and delights in, whom the New Testament directly identifies as Jesus, the Servant who succeeds where the nation Israel, also called God's servant elsewhere in these chapters, repeatedly failed.

mishpat.gloss: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of relationships and society according to God's own character. The Servant's mission, repeated three times in the opening verses, is to bring, establish, and faithfully deliver this justice to the nations, not through force or noise but through patient, gentle faithfulness that will not break even a bruised reed.

basic.context: This chapter opens with one of Isaiah's most treasured passages, the first Servant Song, introducing a figure the LORD upholds, delights in, and fills with His Spirit to bring justice to the nations, gently, without crushing the weak or extinguishing the faltering. The chapter then turns painfully honest: the very nation called to be God's servant and light to the world is itself blind and deaf, plundered because of its own disobedience.
basic.hermeneutics: Isaiah elsewhere calls the nation Israel God's servant, but this Servant is presented with a mission to Israel itself, opening blind eyes and freeing prisoners, suggesting a figure distinct from and greater than the nation, someone who succeeds where Israel failed. The New Testament resolves the question directly: Matthew 12:15-21 quotes this very passage and applies it explicitly to Jesus.
basic.originalLanguages: The chapter's central figure is the {{ebed}}, the Servant, upheld and chosen by God, whose mission is to bring {{mishpat}}, justice, to the nations, a word repeated three times in the opening verses alone. The chapter's tragic turn shows Israel itself failing to embody either the Servant's justice or its own calling as God's covenant partner, blind and deaf despite having eyes and ears.
basic.typology: The gentle Servant who will not break a bruised reed or snuff out a smoldering wick anticipates Jesus' whole pattern of ministry, healing the broken and encouraging the faltering rather than crushing them. Israel's failure as a blind, deaf servant sets up the need for a Servant who succeeds fully where the nation could not, fulfilled in Christ, the true Israel who keeps the covenant perfectly.
basic.christ: Matthew 12:15-21 quotes this passage in full and applies it directly to Jesus, whose quiet, healing ministry, avoiding public spectacle while gently restoring the broken, is presented as the very fulfillment of the Servant Song. Jesus is the covenant for the people and light to the nations, verse 6, echoed in Luke 2:32 and Acts 13:47, opening blind eyes both physically and spiritually, verse 7, exactly as He does throughout the Gospels.
basic.god: God delights in His Servant and entrusts Him with a mission of justice carried out through gentleness rather than force, refusing to share His glory with any idol. He is also painfully honest about His own people's failure, naming their blindness and deafness plainly rather than excusing it, while still working through the ideal Servant to accomplish what Israel could not.
basic.crossReferences: Matthew 12:15-21 quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 directly and applies it to Jesus' ministry. Luke 2:32 calls Jesus 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles,' echoing verse 6. Acts 13:47 applies the same 'light to the nations' language to the apostolic mission. John 8:12 has Jesus declare Himself the light of the world.
basic.oneStory: This chapter introduces the Servant who will succeed at exactly the mission Israel, God's other servant, failed to fulfill: bringing justice and light to the nations gently and faithfully. That Servant is revealed in the New Testament to be Jesus, who fulfills this song word for word, opening blind eyes and refusing to crush the weak, accomplishing what neither Israel nor any human effort could achieve.

context.literary: Structured in six movements, beginning with the first Servant Song, then widening into a hymn of praise, a description of the LORD roused to decisive action, and closing with a sobering contrast between the Servant's mission and Israel's actual blindness and plunder.
context.historical: Addressed to exiles in Babylon who needed to hear both hope, a coming Servant who will bring justice and light, and honest diagnosis, that their own disobedience is why they are currently plundered and captive.
context.canonical: The first of four Servant Songs in Isaiah, 42:1-9, 49:1-6, 50:4-9, and 52:13-53:12, that together form one of the Old Testament's clearest windows into the person and mission of the Messiah, directly quoted and applied to Jesus in the New Testament.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To introduce the Servant through whom the LORD will accomplish justice and light for the nations, gently and faithfully, and to expose honestly that Israel, though called to this very role, has proven blind and deaf, unable to fulfill it in its own strength.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's chosen Servant and His mission, and diagnoses Israel's specific historical failure; it is not a general prescription for how nations should behave. Its abiding principle is that God's justice comes through gentle, patient faithfulness rather than force, and that human failure to keep God's calling requires a Servant who succeeds on our behalf.

debates.question: Is the Servant of verses 1 to 9 the nation Israel collectively, or an individual figure distinct from the nation?

views.label: A collective or representative figure for Israel
views.case: Isaiah elsewhere explicitly calls Israel 'My servant,' 41:8, 44:1, so this Servant may represent the nation, or an ideal, faithful remnant within it, called to be a light to the other nations.

views.label: An individual figure distinct from and greater than the nation
views.case: This Servant is given a mission to Israel itself, opening its blind eyes, verses 6 to 7, and later Servant Songs describe Him restoring Israel, 49:5-6, which requires someone distinct from the nation being restored; the New Testament resolves this by identifying the Servant as Jesus.

debates.bearing: The New Testament's direct application of this very passage to Jesus in Matthew 12:15-21 settles the question for Christian reading: whatever partial sense in which Israel bore this calling and failed it, the Servant who fully succeeds is the individual Messiah, Jesus Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The Servant, {{ebed}}, is chosen and upheld by God to bring {{mishpat}}, justice, to the nations, a mission repeated three times in the opening verses to underline its centrality. Yet the chapter's later movement turns this same word 'servant' with bitter irony onto Israel, blind and deaf despite being God's own covenant partner, exposing the gap between the calling and the nation's failure to keep it.

typology.type: The Servant who will not break a bruised reed or extinguish a smoldering wick
typology.fulfillment: Jesus' entire pattern of gentle, restorative ministry toward the broken and faltering, Matthew 12:15-21.

typology.type: The Servant as a covenant for the people and a light to the nations
typology.fulfillment: Christ, called a light for revelation to the Gentiles, Luke 2:32, and the mission carried forward by the apostles, Acts 13:47.

study.christ: Matthew 12:15-21 quotes this Servant Song in full, applying it directly to Jesus, whose quiet, non-confrontational ministry of healing embodies exactly the Servant's gentleness, refusing to break a bruised reed or snuff out a smoldering wick. Jesus is the covenant for the people and light to the nations, verse 6, the same identity Luke 2:32 and Acts 13:47 give Him, and He alone fully opens blind eyes and frees captives, verse 7, both physically in His earthly ministry and spiritually through the gospel.
study.god: God delights in His chosen Servant and entrusts Him with establishing justice through patient, gentle faithfulness rather than noise or force, refusing to share His glory with any idol. He is equally honest about His people's own failure to see and hear, naming their blindness plainly, yet He continues working through the ideal Servant to accomplish exactly what Israel in its own strength could not.

crossReferences.note: Quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 in full and applies it directly to Jesus' quiet, healing ministry.
Simeon calls the infant Jesus 'a light for revelation to the Gentiles,' echoing the Servant's mission in verse 6.
Paul and Barnabas apply the same light-to-the-nations mission to their own apostolic preaching, grounded in this Servant Song.
Jesus declares Himself the light of the world, fulfilling the mission given to the Servant in verse 6.

study.oneStory: This chapter introduces the Servant appointed to succeed at the very mission Israel, God's other servant, could not fulfill: bringing gentle, patient justice and light to the nations. The New Testament identifies that Servant as Jesus, who fulfills this song precisely, opening blind eyes and refusing to crush the weak, accomplishing in Himself what neither Israel's calling nor human effort could achieve alone.

sermon.title: A Bruised Reed He Will Not Break
sermon.why: Keller preaches directly through Isaiah 42:1-9, the first Servant Song at the heart of this chapter, showing how Isaiah's mysterious Servant, depicted as a servant king, a healing king, and a suffering king, is identified in the New Testament as Jesus Christ.

quiz.q: What kind of justice-bringer does the Servant Song describe in verses 1 to 4?

options.text: A gentle Servant who will not break a bruised reed or extinguish a smoldering wick, yet will faithfully establish justice.
options.note: Verses 2 to 4 describe exactly this combination of gentleness and unshakeable faithfulness.

options.text: A loud, forceful ruler who crushes all opposition immediately.
options.note: The opposite; the Servant specifically will not cry out or raise His voice in the streets.

options.text: A distant figure uninterested in the weak or broken.
options.note: The Servant's defining trait is tender care for the bruised and faltering, not indifference.

options.text: A political leader focused solely on military conquest.
options.note: The Servant's mission is described as bringing justice, not military conquest, and through gentleness rather than force.

quiz.q: What mission is given to the Servant in verses 6 to 7?

options.text: To be a covenant for the people and a light to the nations, opening blind eyes and freeing prisoners.
options.note: Verses 6 to 7 state this mission directly.

options.text: To conquer foreign nations through military force.
options.note: The opposite; the mission described is covenant, light, healing, and liberation, not conquest.

options.text: To build a new temple in Jerusalem.
options.note: Not the mission described here, which focuses on covenant, light, and liberation rather than construction.

options.text: To rule only over Israel, with no concern for other nations.
options.note: The opposite; the mission explicitly extends to being 'a light to the nations,' beyond Israel alone.

quiz.q: How does Matthew's Gospel use this Servant Song?

options.text: It quotes Isaiah 42:1-4 in full and applies it directly to Jesus' quiet, healing ministry.
options.note: Matthew 12:15-21 does exactly this, one of the clearest direct citations of Isaiah in the New Testament.

options.text: It rejects this passage as irrelevant to Jesus' mission.
options.note: The opposite; Matthew treats this passage as a direct prophecy fulfilled in Jesus.

options.text: It applies the passage only to a future, unnamed prophet.
options.note: Matthew applies it specifically and directly to Jesus, not to an unnamed future figure.

options.text: It uses the passage only as background for Old Testament history with no New Testament application.
options.note: Matthew's quotation is a direct application to Jesus' present ministry, not a mere historical reference.

quiz.q: What painful contrast does verse 19 draw?

options.text: Israel, God's own servant and messenger, is itself blind and deaf.
options.note: Verse 19 asks directly, 'Who is blind but My servant,' exposing Israel's own failure.

options.text: Israel alone among all nations has perfect spiritual sight.
options.note: The opposite; the verse exposes Israel's blindness, not its superior perception.

options.text: Only Israel's enemies are blind, while Israel sees clearly.
options.note: The blindness described is Israel's own, not that of its enemies.

options.text: All nations except Israel struggle with spiritual blindness.
options.note: The specific and pointed accusation in this verse is against Israel, God's own servant, not other nations.

quiz.q: What explanation does the chapter give for Israel being plundered, verses 24 to 25?

options.text: They were unwilling to walk in the LORD's ways and would not obey His law.
options.note: Verse 24 states this reason directly, tying the plunder to Israel's own disobedience.

options.text: They were plundered entirely without cause or reason.
options.note: The opposite; the passage explicitly attributes the plunder to their sin and disobedience.

options.text: Foreign armies were simply stronger with no spiritual cause involved.
options.note: The passage frames the plunder as the LORD's own act of judgment for disobedience, not mere military inferiority.

options.text: It was solely the fault of their political leaders, not the people themselves.
options.note: The text addresses the people's own unwillingness to walk in the LORD's ways, not merely the failure of leadership.

questions: The Servant will not break a bruised reed or snuff out a smoldering wick. Where in your life have you felt like a bruised reed, and how does it change things to know Christ handles you this gently?
The Servant brings justice not through noise and force but through patient faithfulness. Where are you tempted to force a result through pressure rather than trusting God's patient timing?
Israel, God's own servant, is confronted honestly with its blindness and deafness despite having eyes and ears. What is God trying to show you right now that you may be avoiding seeing clearly?
The Servant is called a light to the nations. How are you currently living out that same calling, in Christ, to reflect His light to people around you who do not yet know Him?

The chapter is honest that Israel's suffering came from its own disobedience, not random chance. Is there an area where you need to take responsibility for your own choices rather than blaming your circumstances?

translationNotes.note: This is the first of Isaiah's four Servant Songs, quoted in full in Matthew 12:15-21 and applied directly to Jesus' ministry.
The sharp question 'Who is blind but My servant' uses Israel's own covenant title against itself, exposing the gap between the nation's calling and its actual spiritual condition.
Covenant for the people and light to the nations together describe a mission both to Israel and beyond it, language later applied to Jesus in Luke 2:32 and to the apostolic mission in Acts 13:47.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the makor-scripture tool for Isaiah 42:1-25. No em or en dashes appeared in the source text for this passage.
